metric_value: -33% (one-third decrease in performance)
notes: Performance decrease documented by IS staff post-migration. Does not include production time lost from workstation freezes or reboots.

metric_value: +50% (fifty-percent increase in management costs)
notes: Increase in IS management costs attributed to NT instability requiring more staff and time.

metric_value: 2-3 reboots per day minimum (most stable workstations)
notes: Microsoft support response to any freeze: recommend reboot. Operators coined 'three-fingered salute' for Ctrl+Alt+Del.

metric_value: 70% complete; remaining 30% considered extremely difficult
notes: Migration began Spring 1996, missed mid-1997 deadline. IS management no longer willing to predict completion date.
Company administrative desktop count running Windows OS for Office applications.
OS/2-based call center clients deployed before the Microsoft mandate.
OS/2-based developer desktops in addition to the administrative Windows desktops.

metric_value: Frequent database crashes requiring hours or days to rebuild
notes: Documented by IS staff; issues filled four pages of edited notes.

metric_value: More frequent than desired or expected; time-consuming recovery
notes: Database corruption causing downtime and productivity loss.

metric_value: Poor; known bugs unfixed across three micro-revisions
notes: Microsoft technical support responsiveness rated inadequate by IS staff.

metric_value: Cache management, referential integrity, replication, messaging, log management, SQL debugging tools all viewed as inadequate
notes: IS staff identified multiple SQL Server subsystems as immature.

metric_value: Unable to service more than modest numbers of end-users; increasing domain engine count required
notes: As end-user count declined, number of NT Servers/domain engines had to increase to compensate for scalability limits.

metric_value: Random packet drops, router timeouts, garbage packet congestion; cause unidentifiable
notes: Neither Company's nor Microsoft's technical experts could identify or correct the networking problems.

metric_value: Monthly customer service level reports suspended due to embarrassment
notes: Trouble tickets dramatically increased; new staff hired; reporting stopped.

metric_value: Exodus of skilled engineers; replaced by junior staff
notes: Skilled engineers left due to frustration. Replacement with junior engineers described as potential 'death spiral'.

metric_value: Business executives dictated both WHAT and HOW of IT implementation
notes: Aberdeen identifies this as the root cause: LOB executives decreed total Microsoft shop in 1995, overriding IS expertise.

metric_value: Business executives should define requirements; IS executives should determine implementation
notes: Aberdeen's core governance principle derived from hundreds of interviews with senior executives.

metric_value: 1995 business decision likely to have material impact on operations and bottom line in 1998
notes: Aberdeen predicts ongoing NT migration problems will affect Company financially in 1998.

metric_value: Windows NT/2000 eventually dominated enterprise; Microsoft addressed stability issues via service packs and Windows 2000
notes: Despite early problems, NT/2000 succeeded as enterprise platform. Windows Server 2003/2008 became stable. OS/2 was discontinued by IBM in 2006.

metric_value: OS/2 is being abandoned despite operational stability
notes: Platform was stable and functional; business mandate drove abandonment, not technical failure.

metric_value: IBM ended OS/2 support in 2006
notes: IBM officially ended OS/2 Warp support December 31, 2006. Platform discontinued as predicted by market forces.

metric_value: Concern over Year 2000 (Y2K) compliance of SQL Server
notes: IS staff flagged Y2K compliance as an additional SQL Server concern.

metric_value: Microsoft selling NT as a loss-leader to gain enterprise market share
notes: Aberdeen states Microsoft had 'enormous resources behind development, marketing, sales and support' and was 'selling it as a loss-leader'.

metric_value: Windows Server dominated enterprise by early 2000s; NT 4.0 succeeded by Windows 2000/2003
notes: Microsoft's strategy of aggressive NT promotion succeeded long-term despite early reliability problems.
